metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the threshold meter and trust-signal specs to use the per-finding resolved `block_threshold` (from `threshold_for(finding.criteria)`) rather than a non-existent 'round's own `block_threshold`'; update the AC oracle in AC-4 to assert the correct per-finding tick position.
findings.finding: The plan treats `block_threshold` as a single round-level scalar — stating 'the round's own `block_threshold`' in trust signals and 'a tick at the round's `block_threshold`' in the threshold meter description. The actual codebase shows `block_threshold` is per-criterion: `decide.py` line 16 states 'per-criterion block_threshold (parameterized)'; `criteria/model.py` lines 55-72 show `threshold_for()` returns the MIN over a finding's criteria thresholds, and `criteria_routing.json` lists 10+ distinct thresholds (0.5, 0.54, 0.6, 0.7, 0.75, 0.95). There is no single round-level block_threshold in the data model.
findings.location: What section: threshold meter description ('the round's `block_threshold` as a tick mark'); Trust signals: 'the round's own `block_threshold`'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'the round's `block_threshold`' with 'the finding's resolved `block_threshold` (from `threshold_for(finding.criteria)`)' in both the meter description and trust signals. AC-4's oracle should seed findings with known criteria and assert the tick reflects the per-finding resolved threshold.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `block_threshold` is per-finding (not a single round-level scalar): the meter's tick must use the **per-finding effective threshold** (MIN over the finding's criteria from `criteria_routing.json`), and the plan must specify how this value reaches the template — either by persisting it in the sidecar's `_slim()` payload or by the route reconstructing it from the criteria layer; AC #4 should oracle the per-finding tick, not a round-level one.
findings.finding: The plan treats `block_threshold` as a single 'round-level' scalar ('the round's `block_threshold` as a tick mark'), but the actual codebase resolves `block_threshold` **per-finding** as the MIN over each finding's criteria list — and these values vary widely (0.54–0.95) across criteria. The sidecar's `_slim()` function also does not persist `block_threshold` per-finding, so the read layer has no source for the correct tick value without re-deriving it from `criteria_routing.json`. A single round-level tick would be misplaced for any finding whose effective threshold differs from the assumed modal value, actively misleading the gate-debugger audience the plan targets.
findings.location: What section — 'Threshold display = a neutral meter per finding'; AC #4; Approach rationale — 'neutral threshold meter'
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section and AC #4, replace 'the round's `block_threshold`' with 'the finding's effective `block_threshold`' (the value Pass-3 actually used for this finding). Add to the Scope section that the sidecar's `_slim()` must be extended to persist `block_threshold` per finding (a one-field additive change), so the read layer has the value without coupling the server to `criteria_routing.json`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the read-layer protocol for sourcing all criteria (met AND unmet) for the completion panel rows — either add a `per_criterion_results` field to the read layer or specify that the template parses criteria from the ticket description; the current `completion_verdict.findings[]` only carries FAILING criteria.
findings.finding: The completion panel 'per-criterion row' cannot be built from the completion_verdict schema: that schema only emits findings for FAILING criteria (findings[] is empty on PASS), with no field carrying the full criterion list or met/not-met status for passing criteria. The plan specifies 'one row per criterion — met/not-met glyph + criterion + kind badge + citation' but does not define what read-layer field or protocol surfaces the passing criteria so the template can render their 'met' rows.
findings.location: What > Completion section (criterion-centric, single-pass); completion_verdict.schema.json

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define which schema field carries the codebase-verifiable/operator-attested classification for each completion criterion row in the read layer, or specify that the template derives the badge by parsing the '[operator-attested]' tag directly from the criterion text (matching the verifier's exact regex in workflow_ops.py).
findings.finding: The 'kind badge (codebase-verifiable / operator-attested)' referenced for each completion criterion row has no corresponding schema field in completion_verdict findings. The completion_verdict schema uses additionalProperties:true but the verifier prompt emits only 'criterion', 'detail', 'citations', 'severity', and optionally 'remediation' — no 'kind' field is specified or emitted. The rendering template has no defined field to read for the badge.
findings.location: What > Completion section; completion_verdict.schema.json; completion_verifier.md

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify 'the round's block_threshold' for the meter tick: specify whether the tick uses the per-finding criterion-resolved threshold (the MIN over the finding's criteria, matching decide.py), a single round-level default, or requires the read layer to stamp each finding with its resolved block_threshold at decision time.
findings.finding: The threshold meter tick is described as 'the round's block_threshold', implying a single per-round scalar. But block_threshold is per-criterion in the codebase (each criterion entry in criteria_routing.json has its own block_threshold, ranging from 0.5 to 0.95). The plan does not specify the resolution rule: use the finding's own criterion-resolved threshold (requires looking up criteria_routing.json at render time), use the minimum across the finding's criteria, or use a per-round default. This is an ambiguous processing-protocol placement.
findings.location: What > threshold display = a neutral meter; decide.py; criteria_routing.json
findings.suggested_fix: Stamp each finding in the read layer with its resolved `block_threshold` (already computed by `threshold_for()` during Pass-3) so the template can read it directly without a runtime criteria_routing lookup.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit error handling in the `/ticket/<id>` route: specify a 404 response for unknown ticket IDs and a graceful error page (not a raw traceback) for read-layer failures, with a timeout on the data fetch.
findings.finding: The `/ticket/<id>` route reads from the 'story-E read layer' but the plan specifies no error handling for read-layer failures: an unknown ticket ID, a missing/corrupt record, or a data-layer unavailability has no specified behavior (no 404 response, no fallback page, no error surfacing). Only the within-ticket empty-gate case ('not yet run' panel) is handled.
findings.location: Scope — `src/rebar/audit/server.py`; Acceptance Criteria AC-8 ('not yet run' panel)
findings.suggested_fix: In `server.py`, wrap the read-layer call in a try/except: raise `404 Not Found` when the ticket record is absent, and return a minimal error-state page (reusing the existing empty-state partial) for unexpected failures. Add a configurable read timeout (e.g. 5 s default) on any blocking I/O call.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit error-path spec to the `/ticket/<id>` route: define a 404 'ticket not found' panel, a server-error fallback state, and an AC asserting each renders a human-readable message rather than an exception.
findings.finding: The `/ticket/<id>` route has no specified behavior for ticket-not-found (404) or server-side error states (e.g., read-layer timeout, corrupt record). A user-facing interactive route missing error-path handling leaves auditors staring at a blank or exception page when a ticket ID is mistyped or the backing data is unavailable.
findings.location: Scope — `src/rebar/audit/server.py` route; AC list (no AC covers error/404 path)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: 'The route returns a 404 page (consistent with the audit surface style) when `<id>` is unknown, and a 500 error panel when the read layer raises; neither exposes a raw traceback.' Add a corresponding AC: 'Navigating to `/ticket/<nonexistent-id>` renders a styled not-found panel; a simulated read-layer exception renders a styled error panel. Oracle: E2E tests for both cases.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a WCAG 1.4.3 AA contrast requirement to the visual system spec (minimum 4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large/bold text) and include a verifiable done-definition (e.g. a linting rule or manual audit note) in the ACs or Scope.
findings.finding: The plan does not address colour contrast ratios (WCAG 1.4.3 / WCAG 1.4.6) anywhere in the 'What', 'Approach rationale', or Scope sections, and no AC requires a contrast check. The visual system specifies 'saturated color for verdict/decision chips' and 'neutral grey' for other elements but provides no minimum ratio target.
findings.location: What section — 'Visual system' paragraph; Approach rationale; Acceptance Criteria (AC9)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fallback rendering behavior for the Verification pass-2 section when a finding lacks verification sub-answers (e.g. DET-floor findings, older sidecar records), such as a 'deterministic floor — no LLM verification' label or a collapsed empty state.
findings.finding: The fallback for missing per-finding pass-2 (verification sub-answers) data in the four-pass provenance view is unspecified. The plan says a finding row opens to 'pass-2 sub-answers + severity attributes', but the plan_review_verdict schema stores findings as opaque additionalProperties:true objects, and the sidecar.py code confirms that 'the sidecar slims paths out' — verification sub-answers may not be present on all finding objects in the read layer. No fallback rendering state (e.g. 'verification not recorded') is specified.
findings.location: What > The finding is the atomic unit; plan_review_verdict.schema.json; sidecar.py

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC covering trust signals: assert the round-header caption includes gate/model version, timestamp, and block_threshold, and assert a 'certified' badge is present in rendered HTML when the record is signed (oracle: E2E test on a signed vs unsigned record).
findings.finding: The 'What' section describes trust signals (gate/model version, timestamp, per-round block_threshold, and a 'certified' badge when the record is signed) as a discrete UI deliverable, but no acceptance criterion covers their presence or correctness. An implementer could omit the certified badge entirely without failing any AC.
findings.location: What section — 'Trust signals' paragraph; Acceptance Criteria (all 10 ACs)
findings.suggested_fix: Add one AC: 'The round header renders a caption containing gate version, model version, timestamp, and the round's block_threshold. When a record carries a signature, a visually distinct certified badge is present near the verdict. Oracle: an E2E test seeding one signed and one unsigned record and asserting the caption fields and badge presence/absence respectively.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC5/AC9) to assert the 'collapse all findings' control is present per round and correctly collapses all expanded finding rows when activated, with keyboard operability.
findings.finding: The 'What' section specifies a 'collapse all findings' control per round as a distinct interactive UI element, but no acceptance criterion covers its existence, behaviour, or keyboard operability.
findings.location: What section — 'a collapse all findings control per round'; Acceptance Criteria (all 10 ACs)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief ADR or CLAUDE.md section capturing the three key architectural decisions (finding-centric IA, neutral threshold meter, chevron-grammar contract) and their rationale so future template maintainers have durable context.
findings.finding: The novel architectural decisions — finding-centric IA replacing pass-as-navigation, the neutral threshold meter replacing signed +/− deltas, and four-pass in-place provenance — are described only in the ephemeral story's 'Approach rationale' section. The Scope lists no update to an ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design doc, so future template maintainers will lack durable context for why these patterns were chosen and what constraints must be preserved.
findings.location: Approach rationale section; Scope section (src/rebar/audit/templates/*.html)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (with oracle) for the failure path: a request to `/ticket/<id>` with an unknown or malformed ticket ID returns a well-formed error response (e.g., HTTP 404 with a text-only 'not found' panel), not a server exception or blank page.
findings.finding: The `/ticket/<id>` route is a new user-facing flow, but no acceptance criterion covers the failure path where the ticket ID does not exist or is invalid (e.g., returns a well-formed 404 or error page rather than an exception/blank). This is the canonical missing-failure-path gap for a new routed endpoint.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — no AC covers unknown/invalid ticket ID
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'A request to `/ticket/<unknown-id>` returns HTTP 404 and renders a text-only "ticket not found" panel. Oracle: an E2E test asserting the 404 status code and the presence of the error panel in the HTML, with no stack trace exposed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `prefers-reduced-motion` requirement to the visual system or Scope section, specifying that all SVG/CSS animations must be suppressed when the user prefers reduced motion, and extend AC9 or add a new AC to verify this.
findings.finding: The plan specifies a convergence visualization rendered as inline SVG/CSS but does not address the `prefers-reduced-motion` media query. CSS animations or transitions on the SVG chart (e.g. bar growth, line draw) would violate WCAG 2.3.3 (AAA) and best-practice AA guidance for users with vestibular disorders; reduced motion is not mentioned anywhere.
findings.location: What section — 'convergence visualization' paragraph; Scope — 'convergence visualization + threshold meter as inline SVG/CSS'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the visual system paragraph: 'All CSS transitions and SVG animations must be gated on `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: no-preference)`; the static (no-motion) rendering must be the default fallback.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either document that the ≤3/≥4-round convergence-form threshold is intentionally fixed (and why), or expose it as a named constant/config value so future tuning is a one-line change.
findings.finding: The convergence-visualization branching threshold (≤3 rounds → bars, ≥4 rounds → line graph) is hardcoded in template rendering logic with no configuration point or stability rationale. If review workflows routinely produce more rounds (e.g., iterative gate cycles become 5+), the threshold will need changing inside templates — a non-obvious, scattered edit with no seam.
findings.location: What section — convergence visualization rule; Scope: src/rebar/audit/templates/*.html
findings.suggested_fix: Define a module-level constant (e.g., `CONVERGENCE_BAR_MAX_ROUNDS = 3`) in a shared template-context helper and thread it into the Jinja2 context, so the branching in the template references `convergence_bar_max_rounds` rather than a magic number.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the `/ticket/<id>` route emits at minimum a structured error log on exception and (optionally) a request-duration metric, so failures are visible in the operator's existing log/metrics surface.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new user-facing route and Jinja2 rendering pipeline but specifies no observability instrumentation (no access logging, error logging, metrics, or tracing) for the new failure surface, making operator debugging of rendering failures or slow reads impossible without code inspection.
findings.location: Scope — `src/rebar/audit/server.py`; Approach Rationale
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the Scope section: the route handler logs a structured ERROR entry (ticket_id, exception type, stack summary) on any unhandled exception before returning the error page, consistent with whatever logging pattern the server-scaffolding story established.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify Jinja2 over the existing stdlib+f-string pattern (`editor_server.py`/`editor.py:build_host_html`) or switch to that pattern; if Jinja2 is retained, confirm it lands in a named extra in the scaffolding story and document why the templating engine adds value a plain f-string/`string.Template` cannot.
findings.finding: Jinja2 is a net-new dependency with zero existing call-sites in the codebase, chosen for read-only HTML rendering in a context where the codebase's own proven pattern (stdlib `http.server` + Python f-string HTML generation in `editor_server.py`) already handles the identical use-case without any templating engine.
findings.location: Approach rationale — 'Server-rendered Jinja2 keeps the page a pure function of the read-layer data'; Scope — `templates/*.html (Jinja2)`
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) use the established f-string HTML pattern (as in `editor.py:build_host_html`) — no new dep, proven for this use-case — or (b) explicitly state that the scaffolding story's `[ui]` extra adds Jinja2 and articulate the specific benefit (e.g., template reuse across >3 partial files that share macros) that justifies the addition.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm with data (e.g., a query of real ticket round counts or a documented design decision) that ≥4-round tickets are common enough to warrant a second SVG rendering path; if not, use the small-multiple bars form uniformly and drop the conditional line-graph branch.
findings.finding: The convergence visualization mandates two distinct SVG rendering implementations branched on round count (stacked bars for ≤3 rounds, line graph for ≥4 rounds), but there is no cited evidence — no scale estimate, no data about real review cadences, no AC requiring ≥4-round tickets — that the ≥4-round case is common enough to justify a second rendering path rather than extending the small-multiple bars form uniformly.
findings.location: What section — 'A per-round convergence visualization that adapts to round count: for ≤3 rounds, stacked horizontal small-multiple bars … for ≥4 rounds, a line graph …'; AC item 6

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and declare the Jinja2 dependency: either add `jinja2` to the appropriate extra in `pyproject.toml` (e.g., an `audit` or `reviewbot` extra) or substitute a stdlib-only templating approach (e.g., `string.Template`), and update the Scope accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'Server-rendered Jinja2 keeps the page a pure function of the read-layer data' and 'New src/rebar/audit/templates/*.html (Jinja2)', but Jinja2 is not declared as a dependency anywhere in pyproject.toml. The `reviewbot` extra (the existing web stack) lists only ["fastapi", "uvicorn[standard]", "boto3"]; no extra contains Jinja2. The plan gives no instruction to add it to any extra, leaving the Scope's template work without a satisfiable import.
findings.location: Scope section: 'New src/rebar/audit/templates/*.html (Jinja2)'; Approach rationale: 'Server-rendered Jinja2 keeps the page a pure function'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `"jinja2>=3"` to a new `audit` extra in `pyproject.toml` (mirroring how `reviewbot` extra gates FastAPI), and state in the Compatibility section that the audit server requires `nava-rebar[audit]`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the concrete flag or extra that controls the audit server (e.g., `REBAR_AUDIT_ENABLED=1` env var or a new `[audit]` extra) — either confirm it is created by the scaffolding dependency story (and cite it by name) or add its creation to this story's Scope.
findings.finding: The Compatibility & rollback section states 'Rollback = the epic's flag / extra', asserting that a named flag or extra already handles rollback. No audit-server feature flag, `REBAR_AUDIT_*` environment variable, or `audit` extra exists anywhere in the codebase (pyproject.toml has no `audit` extra; grep for REBAR_AUDIT and audit_server returns no results). This exclusion claim deletes rollback specification work by pointing to a non-existent mechanism.
findings.location: Compatibility & rollback section: 'Rollback = the epic's flag / extra'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `pyproject.toml` as an explicit edit target in the Scope section, specifying which extra gains `jinja2` (e.g. a new `audit` extra, or extending `reviewbot`), so the dependency is declared before the templates are rendered.
findings.finding: The plan prescribes 'server-rendered Jinja2' templates but `jinja2` appears nowhere in pyproject.toml (not in core deps, not in any optional extra — reviewbot uses fastapi/uvicorn/boto3; agents uses pydantic-ai-slim/json-repair/pydantic; no audit extra exists). `pyproject.toml` is an unenumerated edit target: without adding jinja2 to some extra (or declaring a new `audit` extra), `make lint && make typecheck && make test` will fail with an ImportError at template-render time.
findings.location: Scope section — `src/rebar/audit/templates/*.html`; Approach rationale — 'Server-rendered Jinja2 keeps the page a pure function of the read-layer data'; pyproject.toml (absent)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a Scope bullet: `pyproject.toml` — add `jinja2>=3` to a new `[project.optional-dependencies] audit = ["jinja2>=3", "fastapi", "uvicorn[standard]"]` extra (or extend the `reviewbot` extra if the audit server shares that stack), and import it lazily behind `rebar._optional.guard_import` consistent with the existing optionality pattern.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `jinja2` (and `python-multipart` if form support is needed) to the appropriate extra in `pyproject.toml` — either extending `reviewbot` or defining a new `audit` extra — and confirm `FastAPI.Jinja2Templates` is importable in CI.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'Server-rendered Jinja2' templates as a capability, but `jinja2` is absent from every declared optional-dependency group in pyproject.toml (the `reviewbot` extra is `["fastapi", "uvicorn[standard]", "boto3"]`), is not present in the installed environment, and the existing FastAPI apps use only `JSONResponse` — no Jinja2 usage exists anywhere in the codebase. FastAPI's `Jinja2Templates` class raises `ImportError` at runtime if `jinja2` is not installed. The plan never mentions adding `jinja2` to any extra.
findings.location: Scope section ('New src/rebar/audit/templates/*.html (Jinja2)'); Approach rationale ('Server-rendered Jinja2 keeps the page a pure function'); pyproject.toml line 93
findings.suggested_fix: In pyproject.toml, update the `reviewbot` extra (or add a new `audit` extra) to include `"jinja2>=3"`. FastAPI's Jinja2Templates is in `fastapi.templating` and requires jinja2 to be separately installed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Validate the inline-SVG line graph approach with a concrete spike or reference: confirm that Jinja2 template arithmetic (or a custom filter) can produce correct per-point SVG coordinates for an N-series line graph, or identify an alternative no-JS server-side approach (e.g., pure-CSS sparkline or a simple Python SVG helper called from the route) before committing to this implementation path.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that a multi-series line graph (blocking/advisory/dropped/indeterminate series, ≥4 rounds) can be rendered as 'inline SVG/CSS (no external JS/asset)' from a Jinja2 template. Generating SVG `<polyline>` or `<path>` coordinates server-side requires non-trivial floating-point arithmetic (value-range normalization, axis scaling, per-point x/y placement) inside Jinja2 template logic. No prior art for SVG chart generation exists in the codebase, and the plan does not verify this capability or reference a Jinja2 filter/macro approach that has been validated for this purpose.
findings.location: What section: 'for ≥4 rounds, a line graph with BLOCKING as the prominent series and advisory/dropped/indeterminate muted'; Scope: 'the convergence visualization + threshold meter as inline SVG/CSS (no external JS/asset)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `pyproject.toml` as an explicit edit target in the Scope section, naming which optional extra gains `jinja2` (e.g. a new `audit = ["jinja2>=3", "fastapi", "uvicorn[standard]"]` extra), so the template engine is installable before AC oracles run.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'Server-rendered Jinja2' templates as its rendering engine, but `jinja2` is absent from every dependency group in pyproject.toml (core deps, `reviewbot`, `agents`, `eval`, `tracing`, `grounding`, `dev`), is not importable in the installed environment (UNRESOLVED via resolve_symbol), and `pyproject.toml` is not named as an edit target in the Scope section. Without declaring jinja2 in some extra (or a new `audit` extra), the template engine will be unavailable at runtime and all AC oracles asserting rendered HTML will fail with ImportError.
findings.location: Approach rationale — 'Server-rendered Jinja2 keeps the page a pure function of the read-layer data'; Scope section — `src/rebar/audit/templates/*.html (Jinja2)`; AC10 — `make lint && make typecheck && make test` pass
findings.suggested_fix: Add a Scope bullet: '`pyproject.toml` — add a new `[project.optional-dependencies]` entry `audit = ["jinja2>=3", "fastapi", "uvicorn[standard]"]` (or extend the existing `reviewbot` extra with `jinja2>=3`).' Also add `nava-rebar[audit]` (or `reviewbot`) to the `dev` extra so `make test` installs it automatically.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
